speaker: interviewer
text: January 28, 1996, the interviewee's name is Mrs. Sonia Hochman. And I'm Marianne Cable. So what happened in September, 1992?

speaker: interviewee
text: The first day of [NON-ENGLISH], early in the morning, I was sleeping, and my sister, Sima, was sleeping. The grownups, perhaps they were up already. But my grandmother was standing, and she heard noise. She heard running. My mother tapped me at the shoulder, and she told me to get up. She grabbed my sister, and we went into our bunkers. Each one of us, since there were six bunkers in the house, all the people knew which bunker to go to. Everybody ran to their bunker. As soon as we got into the bunker, we started to hear running. We started to hear shouting, shouting and screaming and shooting. And then all of the sudden, we started to hear shooting. With shooting, we knew it was an aktia. As long as I live, I'll remember that day. I was laying and shivering. I couldn't stop shivering. I was so scared. My grandmother was sitting next to me because it was, you know, our bunkers, we dug out a small little spot for all of us to sit. My sister was a baby, she's three and a half years old. Anyway, we hear screaming, we hear dogs running. And we hear the Germans are in our barn. They came in. They searched the barn. And they left. And the aktia is going on and on. All of the sudden-- we figured already they were here, maybe we're safe-- all of the sudden, we hear German talking, Polish talking, Jewish talking. And people are in our barn. And we hear German talking. And the Germans spoke very loud-- you could hear them. What happened was, they caught a family that lived next door to my grandparents' house. The man had asked my grandfather to allow him to bury some of his jewelry and gold and American dollars. And this barn where we were in, had another barn where the cows used to be. And in the adjoining barn, they made-- they-- they hid all their possessions. He was caught. He had twin daughters that were friends with my uncle Shulim And he asked a Jewish policeman to speak to somebody, the head of the Judenrat, and to intervene to the Germans that he has money and he has gold and diamonds. He wants to buy out his children.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the German said, fine. And what happened? We're laying underneath, and they're in our barn. Until they dug out all these things, it took-- felt like an eternity-- perhaps a half an hour, perhaps an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my sister, is-- Sima, is only three and a half years old. And I'm eight and a half years old. And if any-- if any one of us cries, or makes noise or anything, we're finished. Even though we were children, we knew we had to be quiet. I think, if I'm not mistaken, my grandmother kept her mouth on my mouth. My mother kept her mouth on Sima's mouth. We shouldn't talk. And we understood when they did that, that meant we didn't have to talk.

speaker: interviewer
text: My-- they told this to us that when they'll put this, that means we have to be quiet, not to make any noise. No matter what, we have to be quiet. And we understood. Can you imagine? We understood.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they picked up all their gold. And we could hear-- it was making noise, all those gold coins, and silver. You could hear, because they were dropping the things. And they took away all these possessions. And what happened was the Germans took the daughters away, and they let those two older people go. I think the wife committed suicide. The husband, I don't know what happened. [SIGHS] The aktion is going on and on. And you hear-- because you could hear the shooting and shooting and screaming. They went around from bunker to bunker, from house to house. With their helpers, the Ukrainians, in most of the town they discovered. At night, it got a little bit more quiet. And we thought, maybe, the aktion was over-- we didn't hear any more shooting, but we slept over the night in the bunker. In the morning, my grandmother got out first to see what was happening. Then she came and she got us. We came out and we saw half of the town, more than half of the town was gone. Everybody's standing around the building like completely shock, like in shock. They took away-- I remember running down to my grandparents' house. I wanted to see my other grandmother and grandfather. [CRYING] I came into the house. Everything was there except them. They were all gone. They were all gone. What happened was, my grandfather had a big bunker. And my grandmother and the children and him and also my aunt Rachel went into the bunker. My aunt Rachel was married to Leib Miller. They had a little boy, Chaim. So Rachel, Rachel hid Chaim and Leib, his father, in a bunker and covered 'em up. And she ran-- when she heard the noise, she ran to her parents bunker. And my great grandfather, Mendel Dodek, covered up the entire family. And he ran to his garden. He had a very big garden. He had two acres of garden. For a Jewish family, this was very unusual. But he loved trees, he loved-- anyway, he [SIGHS] loved to grow things. He covered them up, and he covered up my uncle Velvel had also in the barn in a bunker, covered them all up. And he evidently camouflaged a bunker for himself, near the well, because in the middle of the garden, they had a big well. Evidently, the gardener next door gave them all away. He wanted to make sure that he would possess the house. Because there were two houses adjoining with garden. And he gave them away. They came down-- somebody came and they said to me, not to me, to my mother, that my grandfather, my great grandfather was laying by the well. They shot him. Evidently, he didn't want to leave and they shot him.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were your paternal?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my father's family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Paternal grandparents.

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole Dodek family disappeared in one day. Everybody disappeared. All my aunts, my uncle, my grandparents. My father was away trying to help other people. When he came back, oh, how he cried, oh, how he cried for his parents, [SOBBING] for his sister and brothers. [CRYING] He was sitting around. He couldn't believe himself. We were like in shock-- we didn't know what to do. And to lose a family, everyone is erased in one day. Everybody disappeared. Their clothing were there, their food were there, but they weren't there. A week later, the Gestapo came to the Judenrat, and they said our town is becoming judenfrei-- that means free of Jews. We have to move to Borszczow to the ghetto. Well, my grandmother and my parents said no, we're not going to the ghetto to Borszczow, since the men were free. And they could collect the iron for the German government. Over the barn, there was an attic-- there was a bunker in an attic. They made a wall-- the whole length of the attic, there was a wall. The brought out the old boards, so it should look exactly the same color boards like they were before. And at the end, three boards would, like, move in, fold in. They would open up. This was like a door for us. And when we'd go inside, we would-- we would fasten it. So if they push it, it's a complete wall. And we went into that bunker. We did not go to the ghetto. But what happened, people started to scramble around to look for homes, for an apartment. This one had an uncle, this one had a brother, this one Borszczow. My uncle Leib, with his little boy, Chaim-- he was about two, two and a half years old, blond, beautiful little boy-- went to Borszczow to the Kavalik family. My grandfather had a sister-- her name was Heidi Kavalik, and she was married to Mannes Kavalik. They had big homes, big families. And his sister, Fagen, married Meier Kavalik. So he went to his sister. I remember he came over and he said goodbye to us. And he said good-bye to my father. And I sobbed. This was the only little cousin I had, because my sister, Sima, and I were the only children. In the war, like in '39, this little boy was born. I kissed him-- he was a blond, beautiful little boy. We said good-bye. They went their way and we went into hiding. Well, they went to Borszczow. We were in the bunker. And in Borszczow, they're starting to make aktions. Every week, they come where they grab for slave labor camp or they come to grab you. But an aktion-- and then the Germans in our vicinity didn't even bother anymore to take them away, because to Belzec. I'm sorry, I'll have to tell you-- they took them to Borszczow, all the people from the first aktion. They took them to Borszczow, and they put them into cattle trains. And they took them to Belzec.

words.text: two and a half years old,

speaker: interviewer
text: This was when the town was declared judenfrei?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was the aktia, that I told you about, the big aktion that they had in our town, where they took my grandparents away. But I failed to tell you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was about 19--

speaker: interviewee
text: This was the first day of [NON-ENGLISH], 1942. They took him to Belzec and there they gassed him with Zyclon gas. Belzec was the first concentration camp that they were experimenting with Zyclon gas. Anyway, the kids-- people started to come back to our town. Because if you were a young boy or a young girl, your parents are gone. Or if you're an older person, you have nothing. And you see the aktions going on. Maybe a peasant, maybe somebody in the villages, maybe somebody will give you a chance to hide, to give you a piece of bread, to save you. All you wanted was to lay someplace quiet and a little barn in a hole. You didn't want anything. You didn't bother anybody. All you wanted to be is left alone and to live. You didn't want anything else. They started to come back to our town. They started to make aktions in our town. The Ukrainian police used to go around and look and search. One day my grandmother was downstairs. She didn't have a chance to come up to the bunker.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she saw the things that the Germans and the Ukrainian police were running. She put on a shawl and she started to walk out. Her house was surrounded with a gate. And she had-- they had two big doors, like you know, for a horse and wagon. They would open up. So she opened up-- she slid out through one of the doors. And she went down. And a peasant allowed to hide under a haystack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, thank God she survived that aktion. But next door, my girlfriend's grandmother, Vischa Hollenberg, was killed. He remained in our town, because he was in the Judenrat, and they allowed some people from the Judenrat yet to remain in our town. And [SIGHS] we saw that things were getting progressively very bad. And we don't know really what's going to happen. So my grandfather went to Bilcze. Bilcze was a village-- a big village. In fact, that was where my grandmother was born in that village. There was a Jewish doctor and a Jewish dentist there. Doctor's name was Bodian, and the dentist's name was Meinrich. They came, I think, from Chernitsi, if I'm not mistake. And they were in Blicze, and the Ukrainian police allowed them to stay because they needed-- there were no hospitals. Only in big towns were hospitals. In the villages, there were no hospitals, and they needed doctors.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they allowed them to stay. And he was looking for a place where to hide his family. So he said, you know, he says, there is a grotto in Bilcze. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is in 19-- this is in 19-- this is 1942, the end of '42, like in the winter or '42, December, something like that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] November, December in '42, the end of '42. Was it a lot of snow already?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my God.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cold winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cold-- bitter cold. But, we were still in our attic. We still had pillows and blankets, you know, from feathers. And my father was still going around, and we still had some bread, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did any of you form an associates, townspeople who your father did business with, did they ever help you at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'll get to that. Not-- not at this particular time. A year later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not at this particular time.

speaker: interviewee
text: A year later. What happened was my grandparents-- my grandfather and my uncle Nisel come back and they say that they found a place for us. We should get dressed, we should collect our possessions, and we should go to the grotto. In Blicze, there was a grotto. This grotto was for tourists. Tourists used to come from all over Poland to see the wonders of nature.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a large cave you are describing?

speaker: interviewee
text: A large cave, but this grotto had streets and they had a guide. And it was a small place. It was all made out of rocks. There were tall places and small places. It was very huge. The [NON-ENGLISH] family came in, the [NON-ENGLISH] family came in. And we went in, and in fact, some people from the Judenrat came. And my aunt [NON-ENGLISH] and her son, [NON-ENGLISH]. And Sharma went in, my grandfather's nephew [NON-ENGLISH] was a very well-to-do man. He had an estate. He came in. And we came in. And you come in to a grotto, it's completely dark-- you don't see nothing. The earth-- the floor is muddy earth that if you put your foot in, it sucks in your shoe. But we're looking for a place to be safe. So only bats, I guess, live in grottoes, but we decided we're going to make a home for ourselves there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any leader in this expedition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the leader?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to tell you, my uncles, my mother's brothers. My father and my uncle Nisel-- I have to say that he was really the leader. And my uncle Shulim, and my father, and they were very-- and my Uncle Sam was a young, little boy, but they were very able men. And they had such imagination. You know, we came into this grotto, and there's no water in the grotto. And people without water can't live. So we would take pots or cans and put them near the rocks. And water would drip in, you know, and we would get those drippings. And we had problems with cooking, because they-- the smoke would back up on us, although the grotto was very big. So one day, my uncle Nisel came back-- and the men were still free. They did not come in. They would just come at night and bring us provisions, food, and then go back. They were still free. They still had horses and wagons, and they were still free. They came back and they said, my uncle Nisel really said it, that he's afraid. And he spoke to my uncle Shulim, that he thinks that we should make another exit in this grotto-- dig up and look to try to make another exit in case they come in from the front. That we should have another exit to escape. And we should try to make in the grotto bunkers, so when they-- police and the Germans-- come, that the guide wouldn't know about those places. So what happened, they start opening, they dug in, and they made-- they dug through, and they found some other-- you know, place that was not on the map. There was no name on it, that nobody ever knew about it. And they started to dig an exit there. And they dug through. They dug one and it-- it collapsed and the dirt came down on them. They dug out another exit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have tools?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. They dug out 100 feet high. And my uncle Nisel brought a tin oven and tin. And my uncle Shulim, he was the engineer of all these excavations. I have to tell you that you cannot believe yourself what these boys did. My uncle Nisel used to come at night to help him dig. My uncle Nisel was a very able man. He was an Uhlan in the cavalry, the Polish cavalry. And only the best and the brightest they took for that. Very handsome man. So was my father. They were such good-looking men. Anyway, my uncle Nisel used to come with my father, and they used to help at night, Shulim and the other men dig that exit. And they dug through. They took from tin, they made a funnel. And at night, they would let that-- they would let the-- we would cook at night, so the smoke would go out. They made a very big cover with a chain. And they put a lot of dirt on it because it was in the middle of the field. This grotto was very huge. In case in the spring, when the farmers will start to dig the field, you know, that they shouldn't find it. So they made it very heavy. And we were secure.

speaker: interviewer
text: But what happened? [SIGHS] One winter, there was a big estate there. A German, the overseer from the estate, passed through near the grotto, and he saw a potato outside. And he told the Gestapo that there are people living in the grotto. He didn't even tell the Ukrainian police because he thought that the Ukrainian police were bought off-- that they knew about us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was sleeping very, very quietly, very comfortable. We all made our-- from wood, we made beds with straw. We had some pillows. We still had all belongings because we were-- we still had things. All of the sudden, I'm sleeping, and we hear [NON-ENGLISH] come run. [NON-ENGLISH] was with us, too. He comes running and he says, the Gestapo is here. I hear Germans. And he runs right under my mother's bed with his daughter and the wife. My mother's standing with Sima, with the baby. There's no more room for her. So she had jewelry on her neck, a bag of jewelry. She took off the bag of jewelry, put it on my neck, and she pushed me under. And all of a sudden, she's standing and there's no place to go. My grandmother, I have to tell you about my courageous grandmother. In order to give us children to hide, she went out and she spoke to the Germans. And I'll hear her speaking. As long as I live, I'll hear her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said to them-- in German, she spoke fluent German-- what do you want from us? Why do you come to search for us? We're living here like bats. We don't bother anybody. So one-- one person, a German, was yelling there, [NON-ENGLISH] director, whatever his name is, who's talking there so loud? My grandmother tried to give us children a chance to hide.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, they pushed my grandmother on the side. They got a hold of my grandmother's sister, [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH]. And all of a sudden, I saw black boots standing in front of my mother. And they were taking my mother and my sister, Sima, away. And I'm laying underneath the bed. And I'm scared, and I'm frightened, and I see that my mother is being taken away. And I'm afraid even to cry. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: They took my mother, and my sister, and my grandmother, my uncle Sam, [NON-ENGLISH], my aunt [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH] And they started to walk them out of the grotto. They gave the people [SIGHS] [NON-ENGLISH]-- those are like-- like lights, but you were holding it with a handle, you know? Lanterns?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lanterns, yes. And they told them to hold it. My grandmother saw they were walking. She knocked the light out of my Uncle Sam's hand. And she made a motion to him. And he realized what she was talking, and he slipped under it. Because you had to walk, it was high places, low places. And it was like-- it's almost like a-- like a-- like a zig-zag, you know. There's all kinds of different, little holes that you could creep into. And he disappeared behind some rocks. And he disappeared. My grandmother saw that she had a chance, so she disappeared, too. But my mother, carrying my sister, she couldn't. And my aunt with her little boy couldn't, either. And [NON-ENGLISH], they kept right away in the front because they made him go out. They held him in the front with guns. Oh, God. [SIGHS] And the veterinarian, they found him-- they caught him, too, Wintrop. Anyway, he also ran away. The Gestapo walked out with the people, and they think that they'll send the Ukrainian police to clean up after them. They figured there were other people hiding there. The Ukrainian police will take care of any of that.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people were there altogether?

speaker: interviewee
text: We must have been about 20, 25 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were under the bed?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was under the bed with three other people. Maybe there were 15 people, 20 people, yeah, because there was the [NON-ENGLISH] family and the [NON-ENGLISH] family. In that direction of the-- they did not-- in that direction of the bunkers, they didn't go. What happened was [SIGHS] they came on our side. And they saw people, so they stopped at that side. This is my grandmother went out to talk to them. Anyway, the other people, I don't know, their bunker, they-- they didn't. Or maybe they hid themselves. My uncle Shulim, the one that did all these excavation, tried to get through to-- because those bunkers were made. He knew that he could push through a wall. He got stuck in that wall, and he had a nervous shock. My aunt Yetta, and my aunt Chana were also under the bed. And Sharma was under the bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And [NON-ENGLISH], that people-- and other people were starting to come out. And I'm laying on the bed. Herr [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH] went out, and I'm still laying under the bed. And I hear my aunt Yetta saying, [NON-ENGLISH]-- they used to call me Shunia at home, like an endearment, you know-- Sonia would be Shunia. [NON-ENGLISH], where are you? And I said, I'm here, Yetta. And she said, why didn't you come out? [NON-ENGLISH]. So I said, I'm afraid-- they took my mother away. And I'm crying.

speaker: interviewee
text: She says, don't worry, Shunia. Whatever will happen to me will happen to you. Don't worry, I'll take care of you. My father is in town. He thinks that we're safe. Because we thought that they took them out immediately and they shot them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, we have to open up a big exit. My aunt Chana was the first one up there. They pushed me up, then my cousin Sharma went up, and my aunt Yetta. And they-- and-- and who remained? It took us a long time. She couldn't open up, and she's yelling for her brother. And her brother is paralyzed-- he's in shock, can't move.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of the sudden, we hear my Uncle Sam is back and the veterinarian. And I hear my grandmother is alive, too. My aunts are excited. Their mother is alive, and I'm still crying. My mother, they must have shot her. Anyway, it went through a few hours until we got through. And this was our lock because had we come out during the day, the Ukrainian police were watching the exit. The first entrance, they would have caught us. This was night already. We came out at night. And my grandmother in order not to endanger our lives pulled out her sick-- [NON-ENGLISH] helped to lift [NON-ENGLISH] up, and she's carrying a man of 20 years old. But he's paralyzed. He's in shock, and she's dragging him. And she and her son-- she didn't want to endanger our life-- she told us to go one way, told my uncle Sam to go and tell my father and my grandfather and my uncle Nisel what's happened. I and Chana and Yetta and Saul went in a different direction. My aunt Chana worked for a woman that she-- older woman, a peasant, she used to do knitting for her. And she said, she'll go. She'll take us to Mrs. Mierveldt.

speaker: interviewer
text: All of the sudden, we hear the Ukrainian police. You're running like on an open field?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it's in the field. It's in the field. My aunt told us to lay down. The police passed by, they didn't see us. And we started to run again. But they caught my grandmother, and my uncle Shulim. And my grandmother, the Ukrainian police recognized my grandmother because my grandmother had a big home. And my grandfather used to pay them off. And they knew my grandmother-- they knew about my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she said, you know, my husband is Zaider Stermer. And you asked for a bicycle and he has already a bicycle for you for your son. So what happened was-- and he didn't want to let her go at first. He pulled her over to the entrance. And he said, show us where-- because he says, two days ago, we went there with a guide, and we couldn't find you. We couldn't find you. Where were you hiding? Come and show us. My grandmother says, I'm an old woman. What do I know? The children took me. I don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And look, I have a sick son. And please-- anyway, she pleaded and she cried. And she said, my husband will pay you. Anyway, they let her go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a minute.
